title: Red wine cold-process soap
subtitle: A russet-toned bar made with red wine in the lye solution
excerpt: A cold-process soap that replaces the water in the lye solution with red wine. The wine's tannins and antioxidants remain in trace amounts in the finished bar; the alcohol burns off during saponification. After the 4 to 6-week cure the bar is a warm terracotta or coffee-brown depending on the wine used.
sourceNotes: SAP values: olive oil 0.134, coconut oil 0.190, castor oil 0.128, shea butter 0.128. 5% superfat; red wine used at 33% of oil weight as the lye liquid. Technique of using wine as the lye liquid documented in Susan Miller Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997), notes on liquid substitutions. Sugar content in wine accelerates trace; recommendation to chill wine and use a slow pour follows standard soaper practice.

recipe.batchNotes: Run oil weight changes through a lye calculator. Always use the same quantity of wine for the lye solution and add lye more slowly than usual when using sugary liquids.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Chill the wine before use and chill the lye solution after mixing (set in an ice bath) to keep temperatures manageable. Allow 3 days in the mould before unmoulding; red wine soaps can be slow to firm.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Store on an open rack in a cool dark place. Cured bars keep two years. The colour holds well in dark storage; direct sunlight will fade it over months. Discard on signs of DOS or a sharp smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: The reaction between fat and sodium hydroxide that produces soap and glycerine. When wine replaces water, the alcohol evaporates during the reaction and the remaining tannins and polyphenols are incorporated into the bar.
The percentage of fat left unsaponified in the finished bar. At 5%, a small conditioning oil reserve remains in the soap.
The point at which soap batter emulsifies and thickens to a loose custard. Red wine batter traces faster than a plain water batch because the sugars in wine accelerate saponification.

projectSchedule.title: Unmould and cut
projectSchedule.body: Red wine soaps are sometimes slower to firm than plain-water batches. Wait 3 days before unmoulding. Wear protective gloves when cutting.

projectSchedule.title: Four-week check
projectSchedule.body: Test a sliver on the forearm. The bar will have cured to a warm terracotta or coffee-brown. A thin white soda-ash layer on the surface is harmless and can be planed off with a knife.

projectSchedule.title: Cure complete
projectSchedule.body: A fully cured wine bar is firm, richly coloured, and mild. Store on an open rack.

content.text: Using red wine in place of water is one of the older techniques in soap making; it produces a bar with a distinctive warm colour ranging from terracotta to deep coffee-brown depending on the variety of wine. The alcohol evaporates during the reaction, and what remains in trace amounts in the finished bar are tannins, polyphenols, and the sugars that give wine its colour. The sugar content also speeds up
saponification
noticeably, so this batch traces faster than a plain water soap. The practical response is to chill the wine before mixing the lye solution and to work quickly at the pour stage. At 5%
superfat
on a standard olive, coconut, and shea base, the finished bar is gentle and long-lasting. Yield is 7 bars of around 125 g each.

Put on protective gloves and goggles. Lye dissolved in wine heats more aggressively than lye dissolved in water because the sugars amplify the reaction; pour the lye more slowly than usual and have an ice bath ready to cool the pitcher.
Ingredients

items.prepNote: 35% of oil weight; melt if solid
NaOH at 99% purity; calculated for 5% superfat on this oil blend
Chilled; replaces distilled water as the lye liquid. A full-bodied, tannic wine gives the darkest colour; a lighter wine gives a softer terracotta.

content.text: Method
Chill the wine and mix the lye solution

Refrigerate the measured wine for at least an hour before use. Prepare an ice bath: a larger bowl with ice and cold water ready to receive the lye pitcher. Put on protective gloves and goggles. Weigh the sodium hydroxide. Pour the chilled wine into the HDPE lye pitcher. Add the lye to the wine in a very slow, thin stream, stirring constantly; the mixture will heat and foam more than with plain water. As soon as the lye is dissolved, sit the pitcher in the ice bath to bring the temperature down quickly to 38 to 42°C.
Prepare the oils
Melt the coconut oil and shea butter together gently. Add the olive oil and castor oil. Cool to 38 to 42°C.
Combine and blend: work quickly
Pour the lye-wine solution into the oil blend in a slow stream. Stick-blend in short bursts. Wine batter reaches
trace
faster than plain water; once you see the batter thicken to a loose custard, pour immediately without waiting for a heavier trace. If you add essential oils, stir them in quickly before pouring.
Pour and insulate

Pour into the silicone loaf mould, smooth the top, wrap in a towel, and leave for 2 to 3 days. Wine soaps often produce a strong gel phase; the centre of the loaf may look very dark and almost translucent when first cut. This is normal and fades to the final colour as the bar matures over the following weeks.
Unmould, cut, and cure

After 3 days, turn out the loaf. Cut into bars wearing protective gloves. A layer of white soda ash on the surface is common on wine soaps and can be planed off with a knife or left to wear away in use. Cure on a rack for 4 to 6 weeks.
Storage and shelf life
Store cured bars on an open rack in a cool dark place. Direct light fades the colour over months. Fully cured bars keep two years. Discard any bar developing orange oily spots or a sharp smell.
